我是 Alight，欢迎来到我的个人空间。

浙江大学光电信息科学与工程专业大二在读，ACEE辅修。

喜欢阅读、偶尔写作，喜欢钢琴、乒乓球、架子鼓，喜欢陈粒，喜欢朋友们。

欢迎联系hpforevermail@163.com. 欢迎留言。

docs.text: 2026-07-22 关于页站点统计精简：删除访客数卡片与统计口径说明，五张卡片一行排开。 2026-07-22 随笔新增子栏目 HP0731（2020-2026 七年哈利波特生贺合集）；新增【体育】栏目（乒乓球/游泳/跑步）并添加首页卡片；全站导航与首页卡片重排为 课程总结-拓展学习-阅读-音乐-体育-随笔-友链-关于；页面底部日期改为左对齐；关于页新增站点统计与更新日历板；《微积分（甲）Ⅱ》课程总结补充正文。 2026-07-21 新增【音乐】板块（推荐歌单 + 钢琴 / 架子鼓 / 声乐三个子栏目）并添加快捷首页卡片；阅读新增《推荐书目》；《学术英语交流》课程总结补充正文与相关资料（最终论文 + 展示海报）。 2026-07-21 拓展学习新增文档《制作一个MkDocs个人网站》：零基础建站全流程指南；修复各页面留言板宽度溢出、显示不全的问题。 2026-07-21 全站功能更新：每页底部新增创建/更新日期标注、标题下新增字数/代码/图片/阅读时长统计、所有页面底部接入留言板、关于页新增更新日志卡片栏。 2026-07-21 新增阅读栏目：摘抄与推荐阅读自拓展学习迁入；大一上新增中外管理思想史、身体素质课；大一下身体素质课更新成绩。 2026-07-20 新增大一上课程总结 9 门、大一下课程占位 15 门；Educated 英文摘抄拆分到英文摘抄板块；更新友链页。 2026-07-20 拓展学习摘抄分中英文类并新增 20 本书；课程总结分学期并添加大二上课程；首页折叠与侧边栏折叠。 2026-07-20 摘抄栏目扩充：拆分读书摘抄 4.22、新增四本书摘抄与散装、英语笔记；新增推荐阅读栏目；首页去掉大标题。 2026-07-20 精简首页动效：标题加粗放大去故障特效、删点击弹窗/雨闪/乱码跳转、顶栏浅色渐变、留言板加宽、摘抄列表补全、CSP 加固。 2026-07-20 配置 giscus 留言板分类（Announcements）。 2026-07-20 首页动效改版+内容更新：雨闪粒子、乱码跳转、箭头滚动提示、新文章《我的工高故事（序幕）》、新增 Ferryman 与恶之花摘抄、关于页留言板、安全防护头。 2026-07-20 美化首页前端：全屏故障特效大标题、打字机标语、悬停显示导航、点击 Error 弹窗彩蛋。 2026-07-19 拓展学习新增摘抄分类：Dead Poets Society。 2026-07-19 设置自定义域名 alight404.top。 2026-07-19 初始化：404NotFound 个人网站。 展开全部 ▾

docs.title: 最新文章
docs.text:
• HP0731 — 每年 7 月 31 日的哈利波特生贺合集 · 2020 起
  • 我的工高故事（序幕） — 2026-04-17 · While the Powerful Play Goes On ·

docs.title: 🎂 HP0731
docs.text:
7 月 31 日是Harry Potter的生日。

四年级开始沉迷，或许有时间会放上自己收集的周边与书籍。

从初二前的暑假起，每年这一天会写一点东西。

Born forty years ago in Godric's Hollow, he had the same green eyes as his mother;

Thirty-nine years ago, a sleeping infant in a blanket and a letter were left at the Dursleys' door as people rushed to celebrate;

Twenty-nine years ago, a half-giant with an admission letter and a crumpled birthday cake reopened the door to the wizarding world.

Twenty-three years ago, with the snitch's candle blown out on the cake, he and his companions embarked on a new journey;

Twenty-two years ago, when the blazing sun rose above the horizon and the spells collided, he won the final battle of his life.

His name is Harry Potter, the boy who lived, the Chosen One, and a son, husband and father.

Today, after countless adventures, separations and reunions, they are in a better world. Now that we're boys, let's light the candles and raise our glasses to Harry Potter and the magical world we share.

Happy Birthday to Harry Potter.

Always.

2020.07.31

I literally cannot even begin to fathom where to start with all the impact you have made on my life. Though we've merely known for no longer than five years, you are still "the breeze that ruffled the neat hedges of Privet Drive, which lay silent and tidy under the inky sky."

No matter how "grim" the situation seemed to be, if you could somehow find a way to put some patches of light through the dark every now and then, things wouldn't always be so depressing. "Happiness can be found even in the darkest of times, if one only remembers to turn on the light."

There will always be evil in the world in many forms, but to never let it dull our courage and optimism. "But in spite of everything, in spite of the dark, twisting path he saw stretching ahead for himself, in spite of the final meeting with Voldemort he know must come, whether in a month, in a year, or in ten, he felt his heart lift at the thought that there was still one last golden day of peace left to enjoy with Ron and Hermione."

In the face of doubt and tension, "that's the whole point, you know- international magical co-operation!" In other word, "we're as strong as we are united, as weak as we are divided."

"There are all kinds of courage. It takes a great deal of bravery to stand up to our enemies, as 'he showed, in every respect, the sort of bravery that few wizards have ever shown in facing Lord Voldemort', but just as much to stand up to our friends."

Death is an inevitable part of life and it's something we have all or will be going through and have felt to the core. There were tears and heartbreak, "but know this, the ones that love us never really leave us, and you can always find them," in your heart.

The strong support of friends saves your life, whether it's physically or emotionally. "We're coming with you," short, yet strong.

"Lately, only those whom I could not save." That's what you would get "if I added root of asphodel to an infusion of wormwood." Hope you all well, "the bravest man I ever knew", and the countless heroic or ordinary people on the journey.

"It is our choices, that show what we truly are, far more than our abilities." And you, Harry Potter, will Always be my "Chosen One."

Until the end.

魔法永存，信仰永在，欢迎回家。

Happee Birthdae, Harry.

2021.07.31

About six years ago, first catching a glimpse into the Wizarding World. From movies to books to derivatives and to English. Two years ago, started sending stuff to mark the day. Still remember using Youdao to translate a passage from the Internet on the first year due to low English level, but in fact one never needs magnificent sentences to express true emotions, which also works well in many other occasions.

When first entered the online world in primary school, mostly on Tieba, being childish and ignorant, I eagerly joined the discussion, mostly with peers, and read lots of supplemental information, gradually becoming more familiar in the area. At that time, there were still many people discussing serious questions about characters of characters or other interesting details, though the most glorious years of the topic would be around 2009. There were indeed lots of things to be read.

As I grew older and wiser, I learned to keep silent to avoid unnecessary conflicts, probably along with many people else. At the same time, more fan fictions replaced the traditional discussions, and more kids, even younger and more arrogant than me at that time, kept asking and discussing without even finishing the books and movies for even one time. Plus, many of them are just interested in those cps or some characters without proper and comprehensive knowledge of them. I'm not saying it's all bad, but find it annoyingly irresponsible to act like an expert or a know-nothing beginner, rather than go back to the books to seek answers by themselves. Too superficial.

I'm the kind of people that would stick to something once getting fond of it. Therefore, I can immodestly say that I have gained a rather deep understanding in the area, by a mass amount of time devoted in it. During primary school, I read the Chinese versions over and over again, over 10 times on average, for every one of the seven books, gaining deep memory of the plots and contents. Then in 7th grade, Miss Huang borrowed my Chinese ones for her daughter, and I was left with only English ones. When I opened them, I fell in love with them. The ingeniously conceived plot, as well as and perhaps more importantly, the charm of the language itself. Now, I'm reading them the fourth time, still being able to find countless points of excitement. Till this period, I don't have any personal emotion for any of the characters, but have learned the complication of everyone and do merely analyze, not judge.

Unfortunately, fewer and fewer would like to spend much time on the books, or to pursue its plain and original value. It is almost without doubt that no more than three among all who read this would see "Weasley cannot save a thing, he can't block a single ring" and continue with a large smile: "That's why Slytherin's all sing, Weasley is our king", or to be amused by "The rare gifts with which you were born may come to nothing if not nurtured and honed by careful instruction… The treasure trove of magical knowledge amassed by our ancestors must be guarded, and polished by those who have been called to the noble profession of teaching", or to admire the hidden meaning of "the difference between being dragged into the arena to face a battle to the death and walking into the arena with your head held high" or the power of Erised, nor would many spend hours appreciating and mimic the classic lines in the movies, like "Tell me, what would I get if I added root of asphodel to an infusion of wormwood", or "Sentimental children forever whining about how bitterly unfair your lives have been", or "Happiness can be found even in the darkest of times, if one only remembers to turn on the light," or watch the Fantastic Beasts 3 and recognize the flying Bludger or the background music from the Philosopher's Stone when Quirrel let a troll in on Halloween, or to memorize a variety of things, such as the character's English names, the spells, and be clearly aware where the character appeared and what they did, and who used the spells on which occasions, or maybe the first several pages of Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone, which contains almost 2000 words, and so many things that might be laying silent, yet just like my enthusiasm for the series, would never fade, will echo forever.

And it's July 31st once more. Holding the pen ten days on advance, and typing them down seven days before, I don't know what to say. Or maybe, there don't have to be more. "A breeze ruffled the neat hedges of Privet Drive, which lay silent and tidy under the inky sky, the very last place you would expect astonishing things to happen. Harry Potter rolled over inside his blankets without waking up. One small hand closed on the letter beside him and he slept on, not knowing he was special, not knowing he was famous, not knowing he would be woken in a few hours' time by Mrs. Dursley's scream as she opened the front door to put out the milk bottles, nor that he would spend the next few weeks being prodded and pinched by his cousin Dudley…. He couldn't know that at this very moment, people meeting in secret all over the country were holding up their glasses and saying in hushed voices: 'To Harry Potter, the boy who lived!'"

P.S. I plan to use the next three years to create 5 sets of exams, each getting harder, and requires a mark to get to the next level, all in the name of HPCHO. Ones who think they've known the elemental plots, main characters and their experiences, and a rather wide variety of details can inform me and we can find a period of time both suitable for us and send it to you, or rather, better hand the paper on the spot. But be careful, because you have only one chance. If not passing, then you'll never have a second chance to the next level. The first paper is not suggested to attempt when you've only read the books and watched the movies for once, but you may find most of the questions for the first paper on the Internet, maybe not the hardest type, but only a little lower in difficulty, which would be really a piece of cake compared with the other four, of course, and requires a lot of English in HP. Also, I've achieved the first 300-word sentence in my life.

Finally, the cover of the book will last 7 years, as one way to note the process.

2022.07.31

In 2023, I wonder, how many people still are there to celebrate this day. Undoubtedly, fewer and fewer.

Not something strange. Even on me, I've attached much less emphasis-- the loyalty remains, but my vision expands. I wouldn't call it neglect, just the contrary, it is a display of confidence on the basis of an unbreakable connection, indicating that there is a kind of true love independent from frequent presentation yet still shines, a kind of feeling that doesn't have to be exhibited in the light to prove its presence.

So maybe, it's not a bad thing. For books, movies or songs, we usually wish what we love to gain increasing popularity, to be universally acknowledged. But again, why do we need popularity, to define value.

Harry Potter is now 43 years old. What children like about the series is probably not his heroic personally, his spirit or his choice made at countless dilemmas, everything so distant from ordinary people, but the details of everyday life, and amusingly, I'm one of them.

So that means, our own lives, ordinary as they are, very possibly have something to be noted down, to form a wonderful story, as long as, we treat them, with more inclusiveness.

As I promised last year, the picture of The Chamber of Secrets shall be shown here.

Happy birthday.

2023.07.31

The end, of another year. Third year of my cover-themed memorial, fifth year of all kinds. And today, July 31st, once again and not alone, we hereby honor the Wizarding World.

One day in May, while rereading the Goblet of Fire, it suddenly dawned on me that, given my current age, being our protagonist, the time for the final battle is drawing near, or perhaps has gone over. It has always been something to keep track of my growth and sympathize with the figures. The thought first popped out several months after the summer I was supposed to get my letter, a little over eleven. I imagined myself to be some of the peers in the book, matching their reactions, their behaviours, their subtle feelings. Next year I told myself, I'm now in grade 2, and it went on. But quite different from Harry, I seemed to be growing safe and sound in a greenhouse, confronting no dangers and witnessing no murders. So gradually, the idea became forgotten, until that May day, when I realized, that I'm almost, or have already been, older than the grown boy holding the Elder wand.

A feeling of distance, as if I'm no longer part of the story, even though still enjoying the series no less, or even more than that boy of eleven. But time works in its own way. In primary school I thoroughly searched for others' sharing of visiting Orlando, planning hotels and flights and then carefully followed the notice of postpone for Universal Studio in Beijing, telling mom to quit school for a visit once available. And years later, seeing a former classmate posting photos of the very place, it simply seemed an ordinary site. Stirs exist, but that burst of enthusiasm had somehow, silently faded away.

Followers are increasing. Zeal is fading. Seven years before, in Grade 4, half the class could interact with spells and enchantments. Seven years after, now in Grade 4, I wonder if half the class have actually acknowledged the series via books and movies. A few years before the announcements of eternal faith were everywhere, almost a trend. But time goes by, and it will be years later, maybe decades later, to distinguish forever from fashions.

But we don't have a Seer within, and it would be a total fool to gaze into a crystal and croak in a hoarse voice to highlight eternity. Now I'm talking about future with the present, so innocent, so naive. But otherwise, how may one remember having a childish, naive dream?

This July also witnessed my first visit to the US. On July 20th I visited the Universal Studio, only the suffix to be Los Angeles instead of Florida. I spent hours in the Hogsmeade village, riding the train in the castle, drinking butterbeer, entering the Ollivanders, seeing a child wearing Hogwarts uniform being chosen, watching dance by Durmstrong and Beauxbaton, experiencing everything I had known only too well and longed only too much. My company astonished to my know-it-all, I was simply detached. Everything remained. Everything changed.

So, the same breeze yet again ruffled the neat hedges of Privet Drive, which lay silent and tidy under the inky sky. But a new generation is still meeting all over the world, not in secret but in broad daylight, and saying, in hushed voices:

"To Harry Potter, the boy who lived!"

2024.07.31

Fourth cover, sixth article, ninth year of acquaintance.

End of high school, plenty of ancient projects, plenty of supposed to do. Thought about rereading the books once again but all accomplished were a few chapters. Thought about finishing the Harry Potter quiz planned years ago but long since seemed to have lost interest. Thought about a bit of extra writing about Hogwarts Mystery and even polished a touching preface in May, which still lies unpublished and unnoticed in my draft box. Thought about completing my book collection but until yesterday no actions taken.

Much news this year. Movies were replayed, but still not passionate enough to quit school for it. The TV series has started filming, promising to be "authentic to the original books", though something will positively remains unchanged. The website of Pottermore has at some point become Harry Potter and still been updating, but all so distant, to be considered as real.

Nevertheless, we've still got something to take hold of, something old, ordinary, not the kind to boast about, like Universal Studio or a magical party, something real.

We've still got the books, and the original films.

Though the reading plan's delayed, I rewatched every one of the eight films, after probably over three years' gap. Full of memories, full of surprises.

The Philosopher's Stone, still clumsy with acting, when everyone unnaturally simultaneously started running in Flying class, and when Harry, Ron and Hermione stared at the thin air which was supposed to be where Fluffy stood, facial expressions vacant for seconds then burst out screaming. And voices of children, so young, so scarce in senior high. After the detention with Hagrid the three sat around the fireplace talking about Dumbledore, a scene echoed in Half-Blood Prince, sitting in the Burrow, talking about his age.

The Chamber of Secrets, acting skills improving rapidly. The first time ever to discover the bonus scene of Lockhart in the end, thus vainly expecting the same for all other ones. Didn't particularly like the part of "Who am I, Hedwig", to deliberately externalize his feeling, but generally nice enough.

The Prisoner of Azkaban, less innocence, but still the happiest one in my view. Dementors were indeed hard to present, but Double Trouble sounded beautiful.

The Goblet of Fire, Do the Hippogriff, This is the Night, Magic works. The compression of plots more and more visible, and getting darker.

The Order of the Phoenix, familiar Professor Umbridge and Fireworks, Wands away and D.A. The silence after Sirius's death, half second of news about Fudge's resign on the Daily Prophet.

The Half-Blood Prince, Fireworks once again, at first thought misty about the role Slughorn plays. Six times watching the ending, six times matching the roles with their actors and actresses, Evanna Lynch and Bonnie Wright.

The Deathly Hallows part one, quite unfamiliar with the plots, and all have grown. Close to the close, the music a combination of grand and gloomy, last appearance of Dobby, but not catching how Harry disarmed Draco exactly, seemed to be taken by forces. Why didn't you tell her. You knew it was me. Never answered.

And the Deathly Hallows part two, almost dark tone all through, the goodbye before facing death so hasty, the moment of understanding the necessity of the prefix Professor. Nineteen years later, when exactly, seems to have missed out. How beautiful a simple "I know" sounds, and how transient the last scene lasts. So, the close.

And the books even better. Magical how a simple word "grimace" can stir up such vivid visions combined with Ginny's face in the films. And it's just one example.

And it's not too late today for purchases. Twelve Film Vault Volumes. The Unofficial Ultimate Harry Potter Spell Book. Snape: The Definitive Analysis of Hogwarts's Mysterious Potions Master. Interactive Illustrated Edition of Goblet of Fire. The Unofficial Harry Potter Character Compendium. The Unofficial Harry Potter Bestiary. And there may be more. Collect them, before they are sold out. As far as I know, for some in the list, I just bought the last one available. But sharing will have to wait until next year.

So that's that. Many like me posted their own form of celebration, and I'm no special.

So happy birthday, as always.

2025.07.31
撰写中，敬请期待。

docs.title: 我的工高故事（序幕）
docs.text: That you are here—that life exists and identity, That the powerful play goes on, and you may contribute a verse. —— O me! O life! by Walt Whitman

docs.title: Beginning
docs.text:
2025年10月21日，出于某种契机（或许是重读cc98的新生指引），我关注了公众号“浙大工高班ACEE”。弹窗的时效性停留在2023年，一篇篇推文点开，好多关，好难。

一个月后看到学长交流会的消息，找云峰现宣的学长聊天，你们会觉得因为上这个多出来很多事然后特别忙吗。忙肯定是会比较忙，不管上不上这个肯定都会忙。那你们觉得你们成功从这么多人里面脱颖而出，有什么突出的素质。主要还是看你能不能坚持到最后。

并没有得到什么确定性的答案，那时还是相当不自信的，但至少，要尝试一下吧。

一天晚上与舍友到大运河闲逛，水面灯火跳跃，我说我明年要报工高你俩报不报。一位很认真地说哎你报名的时候记得提醒我，另一位回答，可以试试，如果到时候不是太忙的话。

这一个多月三个人为工高并肩战斗的时候，我总是想到那一幕。

12月与电气学院的x学长交流（在此要致以最诚挚的感谢），他的形容是团结、纯粹、connection，于是心中又增了几分对工高的向往，开始着手去准备。费了不少功夫找到辅修班选拔的网站，浏览历年题，遵从学长的建议开始笨鸟先飞，“只要你报名表填的是全班第一，那你基本是保录了。”

从那时，大概已经明确，我想要成为其中一员，并愿意为之付出时间。

docs.title: Setting Out
docs.text:
期末周结束后，甚至是期末周的最后几天，已经在认真筹划自己的学习计划。要求有写“数理、外语、计算机等基础课程的成绩将重点考察”，能感觉出四级并没有发挥出应有的英语水平于是果断报了两周后的雅思。在98论坛上找到非常多宝贵的资源，借助Ephemeral老师的讲义学着注册Gmail、使用Gemini，掌握了基本的Prompt Engineering；Paradox老师分享的markdown和latex入门，刚好借着线上支教的机会练习用AI辅助做latex讲义；再加上Ewood老师的NotebookLM指南，都是开学后很庆幸自己预先接触过的东西。

报名表的准备决定从Solidworks入手，对着Bilibili上的网课一点点熟悉零件操作，还尝试找一个淘宝商家3D打印出自己画的手机支架。大块的时间被两个线上支教活动占去（所以一个假期里不要太贪心），导致实际推进速度非常慢（尤其是与真正完成报名表的一周相比）。最后几天的假期稍微看了一点模电，可惜最终也没记住PNP和NPN如何区分。

随着时间的推移也在动摇自己对工高的执着，大一下的几门课本身任务颇多，学业，工高的报名，X-Lab的纳新，学生工作，和更多想要坚持的东西，既要又要还要，总有一天会崩塌。是否只是凭着短暂的热情去准备，是否有能力去面对它带来的压力与负担，如果失败又要把这份动力寄托到哪里，我不知道。

但至少可以锻炼一次抗压能力，在极限状态下究竟能做到多少，还是根本无法进入一种极限状态。不想去寻求建议，那或许等到进了面试再寻求建议吧。至少学了这些都会有用，总有一天会有用，再说，不逼自己一把怎么知道自己能做到什么呢。

docs.title: 报名表
docs.text:
3月6日，星期五，招生信息推文发布，正式进入战备状态。一周时间解决两周的作业，不得已放弃读书会的工作组，《爱乐之城》的重映最终也没有看成。纸笔写下距离工高班开始报名Day7的倒计时。

万一成功了呢。

那我就可以说，这是我19年人生中，最光辉的瞬间。

12号的现宣感觉已经没太有要问的问题，更多在聊天与被鼓励，回复例会短信写春天的小愿望讲的是身体不要在关键时刻垮掉，因为那两天确实有感冒的迹象，硬是用小柴胡压了回去。13号在宿舍感慨the last day of peace，一起讨论做题策略，应该利用周末时间充足尽可能把最难的两个工程题做出来。努力起草一份像样的推荐信却总是静不下心思考，怀着躁动的心情早早躺在床上，第二天八点准时到主图，8:12拿到报名表，开始通读——

怎 么 这 么 难 ！！

具体的完成过程不再详述，一周的课，请了三门，翘了一门，剩下的因为担心签到于是到教室去整理材料或写report，唯一认真上的课是乒乓球，因为觉得如果请假自己的搭档会没人对打（事实证明那节课还有另一个同学同样单出来了所以其实完全可以不去的...）。上完体育大汗淋漓加上下雨决定把接下来的大物翘掉回宿舍洗澡，想不到十点刚打开智云听一耳朵就听到要雷达点名，于是又紧急穿好衣服飞到教室。

前几天并没有熬夜，但也基本算是付出了白天的所有时间。会有一些久久被卡在一个任务的煎熬与无奈，但总体还是在享受不断接触新知识解决新问题的过程。并没有非常痛苦，只会感觉时间过得好快好快。周三晚上回宿舍说我在考虑明晚还回不回来，两个舍友不太愿意在主图过夜，于是独自前往，发现晚上的主图二楼其实并不孤单。十一点左右被保安从研讨室赶出来，工作到两点半左右去简单休息，但睡的很浅，五点半醒过来决定继续，略微一瞥就发现了两三位同样在冲刺报名表的朋友，后来刷98才发现，原来有很多人在啊。非常不明智地选择了空腹喝完晚上剩下的小半瓶咖啡。很快开始心悸、胃难受，意识到这个问题后迅速吃了几个豆沙面包但无济于事，到了八点实在坚持不住决定回宿舍简单休息。十点到常微分教室继续推工程题，开始倒计时，距离结束还有几个小时。然后是最后的完善，收尾，距离ddl不到两分钟完成上传最后的report，打印，上交纸质版，the end.

最后的报名表感悟中，我留下了这段文字：

6 天半，156 个小时，说慢也慢，说快也快，无论结果成败，至少我觉得是非常有价值的。

直接切入正题吧，能够理解这份报名表的初衷就是尽可能最充分地展现自己的资料搜集、快速学习与创新性、批判性思考和应用的能力，但真正实操起来，会很害怕，或者很不敢，去真正按照可能学长学姐们更想要的一种逻辑，先去相对系统全面地接触一个领域的一个剖面，然后一点点思考、打磨，展现出一份可以说是几乎完全独立自主的报名表。各位学长学姐也都经历过，不知道去年报名时自己有没有借助 AI 的帮助，我猜测大概还是会有的，哪怕没有那么多。我想讲的是，那样去做很珍贵，但也很难，进度会非常慢。

但是总时间是极其有限的，竞争是非常激烈的，“并不只根据绝对结果打分”的意思是其实还是会至少在不小的比例中参照结果打分的，因此我会害怕，害怕过多的其实很有意义但单就得分这种功利性的目标而言有些无谓的自主性的思考与探索，会让我落下太多太多的题目，然后直接被表刷，或得一个相对更低的分数。

所以也必须承认——其实是有些心虚地承认——有一部分题目就是先通过AI 得到一份答案，然后在理解的途中去判断这份答案的正确性。我无法说服自己从参考书或网课上花更多的时间得到的知识一定更加全面更加权威，因此我也无法拒绝 AI 的大量使用。在这个过程中，肯定还是会尽可能去把整个逻辑思路以及涉及到的知识去发散性地检索和学习，至少保证能够基本理解自己提交上去的东西。但与 AI 时代到来前先广泛学习再细究题目的学习顺序是不一样的了。

包括与往年的报名表对比，今年这份资料册中只有 LTspice 那道题目中给了几条参考资料，而其他题目都没有讲你们推荐去看哪本书、谁的网课或者哪个文档。我不知道这是有意还是无意的，但这是否代表着，学长学姐们也接受 AI 辅助下学习顺序的改变。

AI 的确已经相当强大了，从高中时在豆包、ds、Kimi 间来回跳跃，到了大学主要在用 Gemini，然后做这份报名表第一次尝试用 Claude，每一次接触新工具都会被惊喜到。虽然说很难能够一次性就做到完善，但至少经过几轮我给它指出具体错误，他是能够改对的。这一点我就觉得非常了不起。

就比如做任务 B 的 PPT，虽然说 Latex 报错确实报了好多好多轮，但是在1000 多行代码里，最后一次调整叉号报错数量从 64 变成 0 的时候，真的就觉得，天哪，这就是科技的力量。当时在主图研讨室真的就兴奋地叫了出来，一种柳暗花明又一村的感觉。

当然，途中也有很多很绝望的故事，比如 Claude 镜像不但被限流而且智商忽高忽低，经常转半天圈转不出来，发出去三四次能有一次给我回复。像这种AI 输出卡住，solidworks 死机的体验，又何尝不是一种新时代的高速堵车呢。

并非在为自己辩解，我其实同样非常珍视独创性和批判性的思考，任务 A中也有提到。包括在科研或产业的工作中，或者日常生活中，或者哪怕是学生工作中解决一些问题，也都非常需要思维的灵活性。但是对于我们的这份报名表而言，AI 的发展的确很大程度上提高了出题的难度。我也同样很难找到一个比目前呈现在我眼前的一些要求和准则更合适的考核方式，去考验一个人的工程能力，因为工程无论是在实验室里还是产业中可能也都在积极探索如何借到 AI 发展的红利。比如到明年这个时候，假如我真的有机会去出题，那么在 AI 也许已经可以把一个项目从零到一做出几乎可交付的水准的情况下，又该如何去考核？用AI 完成有固定答案的题目会变得越来越普遍，那么如何衡量这一周里究竟有没有完全学会题目要求的内容，似乎也只能靠一面随机性很强的提问。但很多人会权衡，包括我也在权衡，在交表和一面之间，可能还有三周的时间让我去进一步理解报名表里的内容。那我究竟应该把时间花在更加清晰地理解一小部分内容，还是尽可能先取得一个大致的理解，用同样的时间优先做上更多的题目。这个界限如何去把控，我没有答案。

当然我是自私的，我努力在规则范围内把这份报名表做到一个尽可能高的分数。我不知道我的判断是否准确，多用些 AI 或少用些 AI 会不会得到更好的结果，但既然已经到了现在，我也唯有期待，自己成为一个 ACEEer 的那天。

第二天早晨一觉睡到自然醒，慢慢吃早饭，然后到主图补课补作业。四楼的主图，银泉的午餐，银泉的晚餐，晚餐后操场的打卡，再回主图。中午在六楼窗边躺椅上午休，一觉醒来接到通知实践面就在下周，随后紧跟着一面。只给一周时间做完两周的功课，连续三周的奋战，也好，只是耽误了小测，期中前会相对从容一些。

整整一个星期的课程和作业并没有那么容易补完，于是周末的两天大好春光也只好搁置，周日的ddl，周一的ddl，周二的ddl，周三的ddl，半认真半糊弄地按时交上，哪怕到已经出结果的今天，当时遗漏的许多知识内容依旧遗漏着。

最终还是抑制不住想要写点东西的欲望，回望报名表这七天，至少给努力了几个月的自己一份交代：

梦幻般的一周，转瞬即逝，完全是转瞬即逝。

这一周过去会有什么变化呢，不再惧怕陌生的东西？更加熟练的AI使用？一点点算是预习的微积分、物理、数电和Solidworks知识？可能有点吧。

但是回想之前写的东西，是一次此生几乎都没有过的体验，给自己一种专心致志全力以赴做一件事情的机缘，去证明，自己也可以热血，也有过热血，也可以为了一个目标，一个念想，不计代价也不问成败，不惜砸上一周的时间。

好像也没有太强烈的欲望去复盘每一天，虽然或许应当复盘，但不是现在。

突然蹦出来闻舟的那句。

只是那又怎么样呢，我承受住了，我还可以承受更多，而这一切，只为一瞬自由的狂奔。

你还要问我的答案吗，你知道我不后悔，无论多少次，我的答案永远一样。

我永不后悔。

无论结果如何，遗憾也好，遂愿也好。

我承受住了，我还可以承受更多，而这一切，只为一瞬自由的狂奔。

我可以掌控自己的生活，对于我渴求的事情，我拥有竭尽全力的能力。

我可以做到不松弛，哪怕我还是更喜欢松弛。

十分努力，十分精彩。

这种时刻，是为数不多可以讲十分努力，十分精彩的瞬间。

docs.title: 实践面
docs.text:
又一次开始需要一周解决两周作业的战备状态，甚至还少了周五一整天的时间。周四下午体素课上收到进入实践面的名单，晚上完成后勤工作，草草收尾上传X-Lab的报名表，回宿舍再次感慨the last day of peace，又是一场未知的征程。

3月27日组队，在一桌子常微分小测历年题中间点开实践面试题，在几乎完全看不懂的情况下迅速决策，投票分组，做出来整个项目的流程，然后去小测。

下午小组集会，认识组员，分工，推进一点工作，然后回宿舍洗澡换衣服，开题面穿的是一件夹克。

一边焦虑一边干活，半竞争半合作的关系并不十分舒服，周日上午写先照顾好情绪，再想工作的问题。

哪怕被刷下去呢，那就可以专心补补前面落下的课，做一做科研工作，多参与一些活动，过一个更正常的大学生活，选多种多样的跨专业，多选几门英语，况且已经学到这么多东西了，学到了Claude，学了许多python，数电也接触了一些，Solidworks可能更熟练了一点，Latex Beamer已经几乎能用了，至少也有过这样一段很宝贵的体验，能够体验焦虑和不确定性。或许就可以自己多探索一些AI或专业知识，多看些书，出去走走，去过自己想要的生活。

所以已经走到今天了，就专心干好眼下的事情，把report写好，做一做进阶任务，把明晚的pre做好，把第二关写一个问心无愧的句号。

十分努力，十分精彩。努力过就足够精彩了。

周一上午准备结题面，早八坐在蒙民伟的教室有些头痛，下课后继续去赶早十，在东1A四楼的天台上踱步，你已经非常棒了，再坚持一下，再坚持一天，楼下启真湖中鸭子的呱呱声此起彼伏，一条龙舟划过来又回返，草坪上有人自备了躺椅在晒太阳，那种惬意什么时候也能属于自己。天台上还有另两个人倚着栏杆眺望远处，分别又有什么心事或烦扰，全都无妨。不断给自己积极的自我暗示，一直觉得面向高考的宣誓很蠢，的确很蠢，因为毫无热情为其竭尽全力，但对工高来讲，面对这种极致的不确定性和压力，我成为了那个会一次次对组员说，我们都没问题，要积极自我暗示的人。

结题面换了一件高中的西服，对着做好不到两个小时的PPT流利输出七分钟，晚餐时刚刚从其他两位组员的描述中简单理解的part假装了如指掌，自此建立一种莫名其妙的自信，仿佛只要有一个PPT就能上台装模作样侃侃而谈。

整个项目的AI占比过高，在十一点的主图门口最后检查文件夹结构时并没感受到太多从零到一的归属感，但最终结题面结束，回头去看，好像也学到了不少东西。结题面一两天后Claude Code源码泄露，如果这时再去出这道题目一定会是另一个样子，但仔细去看整个框架与我们也有很多重合，又有些被鼓舞。

实践面告一段落，下面还剩下两场应该相对会容易一些的面试。

什么感悟呢？好像现在同样也不想写什么感悟了。焦虑是一定有的，害怕我们做的太基础，没有别人那样高级高端。害怕贡献度不高。害怕掌握度太低，没有办法灵活应对面试官们的提问。现在来看，好像也都完成地不错，report算点睛之笔，上场pre的流利度也不错，至少没有太过频繁的卡顿。

当然，现在更需要担心的是大物小测和微积分小测。但实话实说，可以接受这学期的成绩因为工高这件事情而有所下滑。或者讲，我相信这些比单纯去刷高微积分和大物的成绩更有意义。

但真的进了工高，可能就没太有机会去选那些想要体验的跨专业了。法学、心理、汉语言、金融、英语，还都蛮想选一两门课的，有点遗憾。

docs.title: 一面
docs.text:
一直在说一面和专家面更多考面试能力，应该是自己的优势，但该来的紧张依旧会来。四处讲报名表当时不会的东西依旧不会，学会的东西已经忘了，倒也大部分是事实。重新打印一遍报名表，从A复习到H，重新学习当时似懂非懂的题目，工程题搜集更多背景知识，努力为自己的模型、公式、参数选择赋予正当性。面试的下午先参与了菁英计划的开班，意外找到四五个同样准备去面试的人，会上重读98上 Slowist 学姐和空叉学长的回忆录，草草写出一份自我介绍，结束后围住已经面完的同学打听情报，压力大吗，会问几个问题。接近最后一批面试所以煎熬等待的时间格外漫长，找每一个认识的同学获取信息，不同场的面试风格完全不一样，自己前几位又经历了面试官的更换，于是更加没有太多可准备，看报名表，深呼吸。

4月3日17时40分左右，自己的名字被叫到，穿的是与结题面同样的西装。检索新概念，然后马上被带进面试教室，磕磕绊绊解释新学到的知识。问题都偏向随意，更像聊天，缺点是被问到的确是没太准备到的问题。因为写了雅思成绩被问英语问题，正合我意，用一个即便不出彩至少一定不会拖后腿的问题拖延面试时间，最终的自我评价是中规中矩，没有特别出彩但不会很差，加上通过与其他人的交流发觉自己报名表的分数应该还不错，于是终于可以长舒一口气，终于可以有一个清明节简单休息。3月6日招生推文发布到4月3日一面结束，整整四周的时间，每天似乎永远是从早忙到晚。

但真的是很珍贵的体验啊。

docs.title: 后工高时代
docs.text:
清明和清明后的这几天，怠惰，低落，脆弱，空虚感，又不想收拾落下的一堆烂摊子，只有精力去做迫在眉睫的任务，结果是落下更多。工图落了两周的课开始完全听不懂，想不出来。大物同理，对讲了什么完全没底。活学活用Latex Beamer做了一个文献综述课程pre的PPT，然后放着无数任务不做出去玩。慢慢恢复，乒乓球课老师组织友谊赛难得大笑几声，虽然依旧是失误不断被搭档完虐，英语课的小组又不尽如人意，微积分小测复习一半，突然很想读书，于是摸起梁衡的《要有光》，很入迷但也很沉重。

又一次周四体素课上收到专家面名单，虽然准备工作基本拖到了周一才开始。又是满满当当的一个周末，周五下午准备晚上X-Lab的面试，周六上午收到录取通知开始加群加好友，晚上刷了一套大英赛的样卷。周日上午是大英赛，下午去X-Lab的开营仪式，晚上挣扎着把人工智能基础马上截止的lab和任务做完。

docs.title: 专家面
docs.text:
4月13日星期一，又是一次早八有些晕晕乎乎的微积分，又一次到东1A四楼的天台踱步。突然觉得 Magic Works 在此时很应景：

And dance your final dance

This is your final chance

To hold the one you love

You know you've waited long enough

最后一战，无论结果如何，四个多小时之后即将尘埃落定，我没有准备好，但我准备好了。

两节人工智能基础准备了四个问题，自我介绍，加入工高原因，竺老两问，优势与不足，都是普适性的东西。然后回宿舍，洗澡，午休，换衣服，穿上当时为了启明开班准备的正统西装与皮鞋，候场。四个问题并没有完全记牢，报名表和实践面也只是飞快地再次浏览，在会议室外做深呼吸，吸气，呼气，吸气，呼气。学长讲不用太紧张，我说我也不想紧张呀。

但面试却不尽如人意，出于我的自我介绍为了与众不同有些用力过猛，6分钟的面试时间一直在谈论与工程哪怕是广义上的工程毫不相关的话题。面对一边无奈地讲为什么要谈论这些一边又忍不住反驳我的观点的教授我尽我所能地流利输出，然后意犹未尽之时被挥挥手打发出去。

出场很快开始焦虑，自己展示出的似乎更像一个很有想法但缺乏工高班珍视的特性的形象。会不会因此被判定为不适合在这个地方，完全是一个黑箱。找学长哭诉，学长讲专家会拿到前面所有的分数，似乎心中的不安又有了些许寄托——至少前面表现得应该还算不错。

docs.title: 一步之遥
docs.text:
但无论如何该落定的尘埃已经落定了，3月14日报名表开始到4月13日专家面结束，从开始的满怀激情，慢慢似乎越来越淡然，失败了也没关系，坚持下来就已经非常棒了。它已经为我提供了一段“非常艰难、非常消耗，但最终无论结果成败都让你觉得极具价值的经历”，已经证明了自己其实拥有热血的能力，努力的能力，竭尽全力的能力。高中时对老师讲“努力是一种天赋，而我恰好就缺乏这种天赋”，那么或许现在我会把它改成，努力是一种每个人都平等拥有的特质，在某个或早或晚的时刻总会有一个Es Muss Sein的场景将其激发出来；而被迫努力则是一种天赋，我恰好就缺乏这种天赋。

终于可以真正地放松一下，周二晚上自己到in77去看脱口秀，坐在第一排与主持人斗智斗勇，放声大笑，很久没有那样笑过了。X-Lab的培训也正式拉开帷幕，飞书的注册与使用，一些基础素养，有些很熟悉，有些很惊喜，但无论如何都还保留着一份新鲜的心情，至少在这两天中将自己的注意力从专家面中移开，不会去无尽地内耗。

直到，周三下午，4月15日15:32分，钉钉对话框蹦出一条消息。

docs.title: Magic Works
docs.text:
第一眼已经看到了标题，【关于竺可桢学院2025级工程教育高级班拟录取名单公示】，心跳瞬间加速。

没有急着点开链接，我先拿出耳机，戴好，开始播放Magic Works，然后耐心等待前奏结束，第一句歌词响起。

And dance your final dance

This is your final chance

打开浏览器，收藏夹，不知道看过多少次的【竺可桢学院】-【辅修班选拔】，点开【关于竺可桢学院2025级工程教育高级班拟录取名单公示】。

To hold the one you love

You know you've waited long enough

然后在50个名字中，第一眼发现自己，第三行，倒数第三列。

So Believe

That magic works

Don't be afraid

Afraid of being hurt

Don't let

This magic dies

The answer's there

我成功了。

docs.title: While The Powerful Play Goes On
docs.text:
向爸爸妈妈和朋友们分享喜讯，有太多幸运，太多值得感激。

感谢爸爸妈妈在背后的支持。

感谢身体没有在关键时刻垮掉。

感谢x学长，分享那些从局外人的眼中看到的信息。

感谢每一次现宣热情分享，鼓励我大胆尝试的学长们。

感谢我的两位舍友，给了我许多精神上并肩作战的陪伴。

感谢实践面的两位靠谱的组员，其实当时对于随机分组还有一点小担心，但很快被化解。

感谢工高所有的学长学姐们，这段时间同样忙前忙后，为我们创造一份别样的经历，明年这样投入的就会有自己。

感谢xfl的各位，一直在带给我无穷无尽的温暖，其实想要去工高的动力之一就是下一级的小朋友如果同样有这份志向，可以拥有愿意为自己答疑解惑、愿意支持自己的，曾经也属于xfl的学长。

也感谢小猫和小兔，有时候觉得很神奇，明明上大学之前已经从许多许多报道中听闻了大学校园中的个体的原子化，也做好了享受独自去做所有事情的心理准备，却经过种种机缘巧合的叠加，开学两三个月就交到了两个如此亲近的朋友。至少，我非常享受这段时光。

与工高的故事才刚刚开始，跨过这一波澜壮阔的开场，我期待这个专栏的延续。

That the powerful play goes on, and you may contribute a verse.

Alight 于2026年4月17日 北3

docs.title: 1️⃣ 大一上
docs.text: 2025-2026 学年秋冬学期 · 课程总结

docs.title: 写在前面
docs.text:
关于要不要放上绩点犹豫了很久，但出于自己刚开学时浏览类似帖子还是非常希望看到分享者的绩点作为一种参照，问了身边的朋友同样如此，还是最终加上了课程得分，毕竟大部分预置课的目标群体是未来的小朋友而非早已上完这些课程的大朋友们（没有任何褒贬之意），而附有得分的选修课程可能也更有助于读者了解老师的给分情况。

而小朋友如果看到很多分数偏高的分享也不要产生过高的心理压力和预期。一方面幸存者偏差是必然存在的，愿意分享的同学大多至少获得了相对满意的成绩，浏览这些也未免不是一种正向激励，但同时也请记住，一门课的绩点成因是多元的，中学的基础、个人兴趣、性格因素、思维方式等等都可能影响最终的成绩，一时没能获得所谓的高分完全不代表自己低人一等或是笨这样子。同时，绩点作为一种单一评价体系本身也是缺陷重重的，更重要的是想清楚自己究竟想要什么，未来想做什么，自己在未来更需要哪些技能和品质。就像我们每个人为之奋斗三年甚至更多的高考成绩在进入大学后几乎变得一文不值，绩点本身也只是在有限的场景下能体现实用性，本科毕业步入人生下一阶段它同样会迅速贬值。我一直觉得区分努力和恶性竞争的重要特征就是自己是否在享受做一些事情的过程，在感兴趣的任务上花费更多精力算不上恶竞，强迫自己单纯为了分数去做令自己厌恶的事情则并不太值得推崇。

当然，作为自身同样刚刚步入大学半年的我，视角必定还是狭隘和不准确的。话说回来，所有经验分享的帖子毕竟是别人的经验，自己的路依旧要靠自己一步步去走。不过，如果能帮助后来的人少走一点弯路，避开一些本不必跳入的坑，我想就是一代代前辈们敲下这些文字的很大一部分心愿了。

——来自大一寒假

P.S. 介于自己大部分参考资料来自CC98，直接放上怕有剽窃之嫌（好吧主要是有点嫌麻烦）...但是！随着大语言模型的发展我们会发现参考资料的制作将会越来越简单，所以最终回到善用工具+善用搜索。大二上开始会尽量产出电子笔记持续更新的（*希望如此）

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 会，雷达点名。通常老师会在人较少时在课间进行点名，点名概率在50%左右。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
作为编程零基础的小白，lz开学前还是对计算机有些畏惧的。从学期初到学期末，难度呈现明显的递增趋势，从前几章的并不费力，到第六章数组开始略有难度，第七章指针开始听不懂，后面渐渐完全放弃了听课，转而试图通过豆包、PTA上的习题和小测前两天的补天进行学习，最终还是败在今年难出天际的期末考试上。

学习方法上，由于C程有许多细碎且易错的知识点，强烈建议从学期初就养成整理笔记的习惯，无论是纸质还是电子。lz选择了非常普通的word文档，通过截图、复制粘贴、手敲等方式收集起了上课PPT重点（部分，因为后面的课大多没有认真听），PTA题目、豆包讲解与解析等内容，至少在无论是小测还是最终的期末复习时有材料可看。就今年的期末考试来看后半部分的指针、链表、文件等难度较大的部分是主要考察点，无论是题量还是计算量上都比平常的练习或小测要高，也因此导致取得高分并不容易。

个人认为刘新国老师还是相对比较不错的，上课会很认真地解释一些概念与例题努力让同学们理解（效果另论），无论是习题课还是钉钉答疑群都会亲自答疑，部分同学反馈脾气暴躁的问题我并没太感受到（也可能是高中已经见多了暴躁的老师qwq），但那种空有一腔热情却无人响应的失落感的确存在，反过来说也是因为刘老师非常认真负责。

但课堂效果似乎不甚如意，一方面是lz确实没有及时预习复习导致大量内容听不懂，另一方面东1A昏暗而拥挤的教室并不构成一个很好的学习环境，自学或听智云同样是一种选择。

虽然C语言在实际编程中似乎应用范围并不太宽，但对于lz这样没有计算机基础的小白来讲还是可以对编程语言的基本逻辑建立一个很好的框架，后期再接触python等也会相对容易很多。

以及，本课程的别名是"C尖"。

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 雷达点名点过3-4次，一般在寒潮降温/下雨等恶劣天气情况下，会很突然，不能补签。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
tww的课评价还是非常高的，如果预置到可以窃喜一下。总结就是讲得很清晰，课件很详细，会留时间整理笔记，如果认真听下来认真记笔记认真抄作业（指在抄的时候努力理解一下或先理解了再抄）期中期末前把课上讲的题都熟练掌握再刷几套历年题就可以保证拿到一个不错的分数，学有余力如果能坚持做完作业上没有布置的课后习题就可以拿到一个很高的分数了，如果还有余力去刷吉米多维奇那基本满绩预定。lz属于并没有完全认真听下来但至少坚持记了笔记，作业从努力理解着抄逐渐过渡到习题课压着ddl不求甚解地抄，课上讲的题只是考前补天做了一遍并没有熟练掌握，历年题也没刷几套的类型，介于这学期的期中期末都很难所以分数只能说基本满意。当然如果没有转专业需求那大可不必把大把空闲时间用在做数学题上，现在是大一上，去玩，去探索，去拓展兴趣，去了解信息，去充分利用浙江大学的平台能够给你提供的优质资源。

tww上课也会语重心长提一些建议，比如默写定义，比如"书上的例题自行完成"，比如"每天做一道不定积分"，这么说，如果你有毅力做到，那你大概率是top；像lz一样在短期的焦虑后权当耳旁风的话，也不代表你就拿不到一个不错的分数，对学业的时间分配要根据自身情况掌控了。

小测是带电脑到教室从学在浙大上测，两次分别在秋5/6周和冬5/6周左右，10道选择题，赋分情况不明。

以及，tww不喜欢有人在教室吃东西。

至于微积分的课程本身，作为一门5学分的大课它对均绩的影响自然要重视，同时它也是微积分（下）、常微分方程、偏微分方程乃至物理学许多课程等的基础，因此还是要适当多倾斜一些精力。当然针对大学生普遍的对高数的哀嚎，我想表达的是，作为一名浙大人，它绝没有难到无法接受，只要肯花时间真正投入地去理解去练习，基本掌握这门课是一个每个人都能达到的高度，所以请相信自己。

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 不会，但介于老师手里有座次表是否到课一目了然。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
出于lz本人英语基础较好，下述评价在不同人口中可能会有较大偏差，请辩证看待。

英语本身是一个比较吃基础的学科，基础较好的同学无论是上课的参与度还是取得高分的概率都会高出很多。个人认为这并不涉及太多公平性问题，让能力强的同学有展现才华的空间本就是大学的底色，再加上占比20%的小测和期末考试中30%的分数也完全来自教材，只要愿意努力去记背并不乏拿到一个相对较高成绩的可能。

闵老师无论是自身英语素质还是教学能力无疑都是几乎无可指摘的。课堂整体难度颇高，花在课文上的时间不会过多，单词则基本不会去讲，作业也可以约等于没有。会有很多补充性的听力练习，文本本身配上高语速或一些口音、噪音是远难于四六级的，但作为老师设计的挖空还是能够填上大部分。口语方面会有很多拓展的思辨性问题，鼓励同学们各抒己见、大胆表达，同时也会留出足够的时间去小组讨论或自行准备。基础好的同学有展示自我的空间，基础稍差的也有不声不响的自由。这是心目中大学英语课该有的样子，而不是某些老师非常中学化的局限于教材与基础知识的课堂。高水平，热情洋溢，令人发自内心尊重和亲近，可以说，英语水平越高，在闵老师的课堂上愉悦度会越高，收获也会越大。

具体关于大英课程设计的不合理与给分细则方面不再赘述。看到不少人说觉得这门课程对英语水平提高帮助较低，但lz反而觉得通过一学期的课程无论是词汇量、口语还是听力都有了可观的提高。当然如果上课唯一的就是睡觉和刷手机那另当别论，归结起来英语这项技能还是需要投入时间去实践去训练的，尤其对于有留学意向的同学，自己希望达到的理想状态是无论短期交流还是申请学位，机会出现时，语言能力可以不必成为一个需要额外花时间去准备的基本素质。

docs.title: 中外管理思想史
docs.text:
• 任课教师：张钢
  • 学分：3.0
  • 课程类别：通识核心课程 · 当代社会
  • 成绩：91 / 4.5
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

寒假竟然把这门课漏了 www，努力回忆一下。

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text:
• 期末考试（开卷）：30%
  • 团队学习及研究型作业完成：40%
  • 出勤和课堂讨论：30%

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 没有点过名，不过理论课有时候会抽组回答问题作为点名，讨论课大家都有固定座位所以不去会非常明显。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
从何说起呢。

张钢老师有自己相对特立独行的一套准则，比如拒绝智能手机，这导致他至少截至 2025-2026 的秋冬学期没有微信。因此他也会期望学生上课期间不使用电子产品，留出课堂这样一块专注的时间。

第一节课，几乎所有同学都遵守了。第二节课，开始有高年级的同学拿出电脑做自己的工作。慢慢地，这样的人越来越多，大概在听了四五节课之后，我也成为了其中之一。

当时会觉得老套、封闭，但到大一下发现自己无论水课硬课几乎完全丧失听课能力之后，好像也不无道理。

就像査老师上一些评价说的那样，张钢老师身上不可否认带有一种儒雅的气质，知识面广，授课内容大多不会在 PPT 上，以抑扬顿挫的声调娓娓道来，也会与抬头听课的同学有很多眼神交流。虽然过了半年再回忆已经几乎没有什么知识的留存，但上面写到的未免也是一种收获。

讨论课大约 5 人一组，流程是助教展示背景与讨论题目，然后个人问题举手回答，小组问题讨论后每组派代表回答。虽说不可避免地大部分小组讨论题的答案都出自 AI 之手，但在一些来不及问 AI 的个人问题上，一些比较思辨性的小组问题上，还是能看到不少真正自己思考出的想法。思考与表达，觉得这是个很难得的机会。

后期某一节讨论课会有一次辩论活动，主题关于苏格拉底应不应该越狱。人生第一次辩论，还蛮有意思。

个人发言会有加分，参与辩论也有加分，我大概有 7 次，算是发言最多的一批人之一吧。

小组作业是一篇读书报告加一篇研究性学习的论文，每个小组会抽取自己的人物和书籍。选组长的时候同组的学长们传授了很多组长可能会面对的诸多令人无语的场面，过了一年看果然是这样。绝大多数的小组作业最终给到组长身上的工作量往往甚至大于组长个人完成，分工，设 ddl，做自己的部分，催拖 ddl 的组员做他们的部分，把组员交上来的 AI 痕迹趋向于正无穷的东西改得能看，解决组员的问题，改组员做的过于丑陋的 PPT，上场 pre，诸如此类（非特指该门课程）。现在随着 AI 发展这个问题我想会减轻一些，不过同样也是珍贵的体验。如果大一刚入学事情不算太多不妨多当几个小组长，因为一学期两学期之后可能就没有这份心气了。

期末开卷考试，历年题 98 上都能找到，会有重合度，考前让 AI 写一份答案出来即可。

docs.title: 电工电子学概论
docs.text:
• 任课教师：赵江萍
  • 学分：1.5
  • 课程类别：通识选修课程 · 博雅技艺
  • 成绩：94 / 4.8
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text:
• 平时（含考勤、作业、课堂测验、结课思考等）：40%
  • 期末（开卷）：60%

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 偶尔会点，通常是以课堂小测的形式，包括但不限于学在浙大小测、纸笔小测等（但纸笔小测不占分不分对错只是检验是否准时到课）。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
赵老师在査老师上只有6.5分，对这门课的评价也属于两极分化的类型。lz选课时的初衷主要在于课程涵盖的知识与lz的专业方向契合度也较高，可以权当预习专业课去上，于是坦然接受了评价中这门课很硬的事实。就这个初衷来看，这门课基本满足了我的需求，无论是电动机还是数电模电都做了很好的概念铺垫，此后再接触相关知识畏难情绪会少很多。赵老师讲课节奏还是比较照顾零基础的同学的，当然这是在有高中物理做铺垫的基础上，无论是授课还是PPT都相对条理清晰，虽然依旧懵懵懂懂但至少能接受大部分基本概念。

当然，授课的清晰无法遮盖课程本身的难度，刚刚拿到教材时的震撼让人很难相信这是一门通识选修课程，尽管授课难度与考试要求要远远低于教材，但数电模电自然也不太是两个半小时能够讲清楚的板块。如果没有相关基础的话，在需要理解而非仅仅记忆的章节认真听课还是尽量做到吧，认真去听还是能收获颇多的。

作业量并不算大，且网上可以搜到大部分的题目。有一次作业是关于电动机的电路设计，对于lz这种小白未免太过挑战了，于是lz委托高中学过物竞的同学代劳了）当然如果认真去学一下课本上相关的内容，理解教材中几个示意图之后，这个任务还是可以完成的。

这门课程只有八周，因此期末考试会来得比想象中快，lz是期末前用大约一天半的时间认真从头到尾读了一遍课本和笔记，能理解的部分尽可能都去理解了，自己认为的重点也标画了，尽管考场上自认为还是有许多不会/不确定的内容，但从总评来看老师是一定捞过不少的。

总结一下，如果愿意认真听课愿意适当花一些时间去准备的话，这门课还是非常推荐给专业相关且提前接触相关知识意愿比较强的同学的（如果有相关基础那么这门课大概还是非常基础的，毕竟大部分知识都只是浮在表层，完全不要求复杂运算与推导）。而抛除上述因素，单纯为了一个"博雅技艺"的title去选的话，6.5分的评分也不无道理。

docs.title: 线性代数（甲）
docs.text:
• 任课教师：汪国军
  • 学分：3.5
  • 课程类别：必修 · 自然科学通识类
  • 成绩：95 / 5.0
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text:
• 作业：15%
  • 小测：10%
  • MOOC测试：5%
  • 期中：20%
  • 期末：50%

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 从未点名。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
lz是选课时赌了一下退掉预置课选了汪老师并幸运选上，汪老师同样评价相当高，讲课会先用通俗的语言和简单的例子说明原理，然后再过渡到课本概念。介于现行线性代数教材强大的防自学机制，这种授课方式对lz理解相关知识起到了非常大的作用，虽然即便如此lz上课时依旧一头雾水。给分方面汪老师可谓是仁至义尽，上课时就不止一次提到自己出身农村，懂得同学们压力都很大，无论是保研还是转专业都很需要高绩点，所以在自己权力范围之内一定会尽可能给高分，这一点大家不用担心，大家只要平时好好学，把期中期末卷面分考上去就好了。事实确实如此，当然也是因为今年期中期末都相对容易，至少lz所在的平行班均绩是很高的，lz本人也被捞到了上一档。出分后汪老师在钉钉群里发"我和助教老师都已经尽力了"，还是非常感动的。

学习经验的话，理解为主。介于线性代数概念多且杂，不同章节直接还会错综交杂在一起，因此听课之余自己最好做一下梳理工作。两次小测和期中前如果能分别较为系统地梳理一遍，那么期末复习的压力会小很多。个人感觉平时学习顺序应该是认真听课→笔记+例题→作业题→课后习题→LALU等资料，鉴于lz的作业大部分也是ddl前一天在懵懵懂懂中抄上的，后续步骤更是毫无作为，所以两次小测发挥一般，完全借着期末前刷Ephemeral的讲义+难度较低的试卷混到了高分。7填空（17空，每空5分）+1证明（2问，共15分）的题型我们只是第二个学期，24-25春夏的试卷难度过高，25-26秋冬又有些偏低了，未来或许会达到一种平衡。

小测形式是习题课纸质测试，20min，两道题目，按比例赋分，出于汪老师的善意这个分数大概率会比较好看，但仍旧应当认真准备，记住这句话，期末补天时会万分感激为了小测和期中认真学习过的自己。

线性代数课程本身也非常重要，相关的运算与概念同样是物理、计算机等多领域问题的基础，3.5学分比重也很大，85分填空的题型安排也导致如果某个细节没掌握到可能直接痛失十几分的结果，因此应当予以重视。

docs.title: 相关资料
docs.text:

  • 课后习题答案
  • 可以自行参与"朋辈辅学"线性代数课程

docs.title: 军事理论
docs.text:
• 任课教师：吴周礼
  • 学分：2.0
  • 课程类别：必修 · 军体类
  • 成绩：90 / 4.5
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text:
• 平时（出勤、小测、论文）：60%
  • 期末（开卷）：40%

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 不定期数字点名，部分情况下考勤/小测前会在钉钉群里提醒。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
吴老师是很好的老师，我们这届大概是他第一年教这门课，会比较为学生着想，发过一次小饼干（人人有份！因为我们是下午9/10节上课，时间会比较晚），最后一节课展示了实体枪支，会鼓励大家举手回答一些简单或开放的问题，并表明发言的同学可以有加分，同理最终论文做了pre的同学也会有加分。

考前建议看一遍课本，打印一下提纲。

docs.title: 军训
docs.text:
• 任课教师：浙江大学
  • 学分：2.0
  • 课程类别：必修 · 军体类
  • 成绩：95 / 5.0
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text: 辅导员会发的，可能每个连都会不一样。

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 点的，每天都点，每天还要点好几次（）

docs.title: 课程总结与建议
docs.text:
可以根据自身身体素质和能力特长合理安排，lz是零违纪+两次哨兵+班长+尖刀班+表演方阵（抗洪战士）+＞日均一篇的投稿水到了一个高分，当然开学时学长们就讲，作为一门两学分的课程，十几天高强度体力劳动能额外拿到的分数可能还不如微积分期末多做出一道大题。

军训当然有很多不合理之处，所谓的服从性测试，很多官僚主义等等，并不是一两个人能解决的问题，当然可以私下吐槽一下，发到网上要慎重。

如果训练很痛苦，就想想每一分钟的训练都是在为几个月后的体测和12min跑奠基，或许能好受一点。

尝试交个朋友吧，可能身边有些人能轻而易举找到同省同市乃至同校的同学热切交谈，比较内向的话也不用担心，有很多交朋友的机会。

如果舍友同意的话洗衣机要早装，最好报到当天就下单尽快送到，正式军训期间对洗衣机的要求是已经装上的不作拆除处理但不允许新安装，如果不想把大把时间浪费在洗衣服上建议早点入手。

体谅一下辅导员和带训干部吧，他们真的很辛苦，尽量少添些麻烦，入党申请书也尽量写一下吧，哪怕你不是真心想入党入团，辅导员有考核要求，你好我好大家好的道理可以体会一下。

闲着无聊刷手机的时候可以多刷刷98，看看学长学姐的经验分享，初步体会一下"善用搜索"四个字的魅力，但注意在弄清楚发帖要求之前谨慎提问。

docs.title: 教师评价
docs.text: 辅导员超级好，带训干部也很好，教官也挺不错，其他的我不说。

docs.title: 思想道德与法治
docs.text:
• 任课教师：王君
  • 学分：3.0
  • 课程类别：必修 · 思政类
  • 成绩：89 / 4.5
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text: 忘了。

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 会，因为学校有要求，总共点了五次左右，数字/雷达点名。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
首先，王老师真的是非常好非常好的老师，年轻，真诚，贴近学生，处处为学生着想，上课不会限制去看手机/做自己的事情，志愿者等等都是按时提交即满分，为这门课减小了很多任务量。即便这门课公认非常无聊，老师也总能在课上创造出几个能够吸引到我们注意力的点，期末也大概是出于90分的正态要求把自己捞到89分，属于自己会比较欣赏的一类老师。

当然总有美中不足的地方，介于自己是回答问题三次+组长+pre，付出了不少时间精力最终拿到一个4.5与身边许多4.8乃至5.0的同学去比会稍有落差。或许是因为今年的奇葩期末题里自己在为数不多或许能抄书的地方也没有认真抄书，或许只是占比25%的pre同学们集体压分，完全不往高分段去打，不过同学互评的制度似乎也是统一的。

期末开卷，论述题六选四，今年的题目大多比较新奇，备考无效。

docs.title: 身体素质课
docs.text:
• 任课教师：楼恒阳
  • 学分：0
  • 成绩：与体育课同分
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

（2026 暑假补）

温暖的人，温暖的心。

现在体素课都改革了，一定会签到，授课内容统一，所以选老师没有什么意义了。

但跟着楼老师的这段时间里，很幸运每周二晚上能有一节课的欢声笑语、大汗淋漓的时光。

身体素质是个很珍贵的东西，如果高中有不错的基础，尽量不要丢掉吧。

docs.title: 乒乓球（初级）
docs.text:
• 任课教师：黄小玲
  • 学分：1.0
  • 课程类别：必修 · 体育类
  • 成绩：100 / 5.0
  • 学期：2025-2026 秋冬学期

docs.title: 分数构成
docs.text:
• 专项技术（1分钟正手攻球）：40%
  • 身体素质（12min跑：20% + 引体向上/仰卧起坐：10%）：30%
  • 理论考试：10%
  • 平时成绩：10%
  • 课外锻炼：10%
  • 额外加分（参与任意体育竞赛）：2分（在100分满分基础上额外加）

docs.title: 是否点名
docs.text: 会，每节课雷达点名。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
对零基础的同学来说，乒乓球的初级课还是有一定难度的。lz在高考后的暑假上过五六节乒乓球课，练过正反手的基本动作框架，因此上手会相对简单不少，但身边许多同学其实自始至终就没有真正规范自己的动作，而在1to30的授课模式下每个人能分到的单独纠正的机会也是极为有限的，因此专项技术的40分想要拿到很高得分并非易事，技术稍弱的同学在专项考试前可能还需要到辅导站进行额外训练。

具体来讲，考试形式是多球制正手攻球，对面的送球人不限，可以是搭档，可以是乒协的辅助考试员，也可以自己找外援，在最后一次测试时黄老师也会亲自上手送球。在能连续对攻的前提下采用连续对攻，如果因自己失误则对面会立刻重新发球，一分钟达到60个回合满分，需要一定的球速。

平时练习以与搭档对打为主，因此找一个好的搭档就颇为重要了。lz的搭档基础略薄弱，练习时就会花费相当多的时间去捡球。如果班上有自己认识的同学自然最好，如果没有的话也尽量在选择搭档时挑选水平与自己相当的同学，体验会好很多。

当然在考试规定的正手攻球之外，课程还是很有意思的。老师会教授正反手发下旋球的方法，也会详细讲解双打的规则并邀请水平较高的同学进行展示和让同学们自行组队体验，这些技术本身还是趣味性较高的。

12分钟跑和引体向上方面，黄老师大概属于较求是的老师（当然lz本人非常认同这种求是，身体永远是自己的，对普通人来说体育也本就是肯付出就有回报，从高中到现在lz坚持觉得拥有一个强健的体魄比学业上单纯多考几分有意义的多，虽然也能理解身体素质略差希望老师放水给高分的同学）

参加学校组织的体育类竞赛可以额外拿到两分的加分，三好杯、校运会、元旦的趣味运动会（easy and fun）。

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
出于上学期大英四不错的体验于是冲着闵老师选课，但这个班级似乎相对内向一些，小组讨论的时间大多很沉默，不过也不妨碍闵老师是一位非常值得推荐的老师。

很难说这门课真正对所谓的学术交流有多少实质性的帮助，也可能是因为光电学院自带了文献综述与科技写作课，所以一些基本的常识性内容已经了解过了，如果放在大一上学期或许收获会更大。

到了后半学期因为各种事情肉眼可见地变多所以上课也并没有非常认真在听，完成课堂任务之余基本在用电脑做自己的事情。

但海报展示比较有意思，有的组做了非常大的海报，自然给人一种比较专业阔气的感觉，所以推荐做大一些。其实组员准备了展示的稿子，至少一半的同学是对着讲稿在念，剩下的一大部分也主要读海报。但其实一个颇具学术性的课题很难很难用英文让可能完全不了解该领域的同学理解，尤其是有些同学做了专业相关的机器学习、金属催化等等课题，这个时候如果想做一个出彩的展示可能需要对这个领域相对深入的了解和一些口语功底，重点不在于学术性而是表达清楚自己的研究做了什么，结果是什么。如果能有交互性的问答和针对性的讲解——针对性指根据听者的听力水平和对领域的了解程度动态调整讲解的深度——我觉得应该是最佳的状态，包括可以去体会第一遍讲非常紧张，会有很多卡壳的地方不完善的地方，然后在第二第三遍讲的时候逐渐放松下来同时去改进去完善这样一种变化，非常珍贵的表达机会。

当然表达不好也没关系，展示环节一共只占10%，同学们给出的分数也大多会善意地集中在95-100（少数人除外），而且老师也不会根据同学打分的平均分去定夺这部分的分数，所以完全不需要太担心给分的问题。课堂稍微活跃一点，四篇作文和论文在AI写完之后略微打磨一下，学在浙大的线上学习按时完成，基本就能拿一个很不错的分数了。

同时这门课教给我不要拖到ddl最后一刻再做作业，下午2点15上课而我的写作作业大多会在当天吃完午饭打开电脑让Claude开始工作，可惜有一天梯子非常不稳定，频繁断连，最后被逼无奈换成deepseek，交付质量又非常低，导致上课时都没有改完，第一节课花了大半节课最终完善好然后利用5分钟的课间从东6三楼飞到东1B楼下的打印机发现坏了又飞到学生文化长廊的打印机飞速打印出来然后飞回教室最终还是迟到了一分钟[ac01]

docs.title: 课程总结与建议 & 教师评价
docs.text:
好多人写过对卢老师、对微甲的总结，所以重点记录一下个人体验。

蒙民伟楼的座位做笔记非常不方便，前面的桌子极高椅子极低，导致如果要写字需要全程坐直加架肘很难受，而后面的小桌板又太小，勉强放一本课本或一个平板，笔记本也放不下，笔也没处搁，想用电脑要么接受它歪着，要么用腿架平。

这会造成听课的舒适度大幅下降，进而提供了充分的把手机摆在教材上面随机打开一个可爱的app开始认真摸鱼的理由，进而导致后面的内容自行脑补。

除此之外介于大一下学期参与ACEE选拔对于像我这种all in的选手来说会落下至少一周的课，在经历好多次疯狂缺课+疯狂赶作业ddl的循环后，上课开始基本听不懂于是习惯于摸鱼，所以最终取得一个很可以接受的成绩还是要感谢每周做作业之前至少花点时间学习一下课程内容的自己吧。

不过对于一个五学分的大课还是更推荐平时多花些时间，而不是把抄作业的任务留到习题课的45分钟...

期末似乎考了不少历年题的变式，目前来看微积分刷历年题还是正确的方向。

docs.title: Psychological Motivations for Idol Worship and Their Perceived Impact Among Chinese University Students
docs.text: Wen Zhenyang

docs.title: Abstract
docs.text:
Fan culture has evolved from a niche subculture into a pervasive social force in China, yet the psychological motivations underlying idol worship among university students and their relationship to perceived impact remain insufficiently understood. This study investigates three interconnected research questions: (1) What are the dominant psychological motivations driving idol worship among Chinese university students? (2) How do different motivations relate to students' perceived impact of idol worship on their personal lives? (3) How do students with different worship motivations perceive fan culture conflicts' effect on the online environment? An online survey was administered to 193 Chinese university students (45.6% male, 54.4% female), of whom 129 reported having an idol. The survey measured idol type, worship intensity, psychological motivations, fan circle participation, perceived personal impact, and perceived impact on the online environment. Descriptive statistical analysis revealed that cognitive-aspirational motivations—particularly talent recognition (89.92%) and personality/values identification (82.95%)—dominated overwhelmingly, while emotional-attachment motives such as escapism (9.30%) and social-belonging needs (11.63%) were comparatively minor. Most respondents perceived idol worship as personally positive (42.64%) or neutral (48.06%), with only 5.43% reporting negative impact. However, a striking private–public paradox emerged: over 70% of idol-worshipping respondents evaluated fan culture conflicts as damaging to the online environment. These findings suggest that individual idol worship, driven primarily by aspirational identification, functions as a largely benign or beneficial personal experience, while the genuine risks arise from the organized, collective dynamics of fan culture. This study contributes to the literature by centering motivation as a primary variable and linking distinct motivational profiles to differential perceived outcomes at both personal and social levels.

Keywords: idol worship, celebrity worship, psychological motivation, fan culture, Chinese university students, parasocial interaction, perceived impact

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Fan culture in China has undergone a dramatic transformation over the past three decades, evolving from a niche subculture into a pervasive social force that shapes public discourse, commercial markets, and the daily lives of millions of young people (Zhang, 2024). Chinese fan communities now constitute a significant proportion of internet users, and the activities of organized fan circles—ranging from chart-ranking campaigns and coordinated purchasing to large-scale online conflicts—have generated intense public debate and drawn regulatory attention from state authorities (Mao et al., 2023; Wang & Ge, 2023). University students, who are at a critical stage of identity formation and value development, represent a particularly significant population within this landscape. Understanding why they engage in idol worship and how they perceive its effects is therefore both an academic and a social priority.

Despite growing scholarly attention to celebrity worship, existing research has predominantly focused on the relationship between worship intensity and psychological outcomes, treating motivation as an implicit rather than a central variable (Brooks, 2021). The dominant paradigm, exemplified by McCutcheon et al.'s (2002) absorption-addiction model and Celebrity Worship Scale, conceptualizes worship along a continuum from entertainment-social engagement to borderline-pathological attachment, with the primary research question being whether and how worship harms mental health (Maltby et al., 2004; Zhang et al., 2026). While this body of work has generated valuable insights, it leaves a critical gap: we know relatively little about how different psychological motivations for idol worship—such as cognitive admiration, emotional attachment, aesthetic attraction, or social belonging—differentially relate to students' own perceptions of worship's impact on their personal lives and on the broader online environment.

This gap is particularly significant in the Chinese context, where fan culture operates within a unique sociocultural ecosystem characterized by platform-mediated mobilization, commercial logic, and entanglements with state power (Zhang, 2024; Wang & Ge, 2023). Recent scholarship has begun to reveal the complexity of Chinese fan engagement. Wei and Huang (2024) identified six distinct fan profiles among Chinese emerging adults, demonstrating that a simple healthy-to-pathological continuum oversimplifies the phenomenon. Yet even these more nuanced typologies have not systematically examined how motivational differences shape perceived outcomes.

The present study addresses this gap by investigating the following three interconnected research questions:

RQ1: What are the dominant psychological motivations driving idol worship among Chinese university students?

RQ2: How do different motivations relate to students' perceived impact of idol worship on their personal lives?

RQ3: How do students with different worship motivations perceive fan culture conflicts' effect on the online environment?

These three questions form a logical chain: identifying the motivational landscape, then linking motivations to perceived personal impact, and finally to perceived social impact. By centering motivation as the primary analytical variable and connecting it to both personal and collective dimensions of perceived impact, this study aims to move beyond broad descriptions of celebrity worship and contribute a more differentiated understanding of how and why idol worship matters to those who practice it.

docs.title: 2. Literature Review
docs.text: Fan culture has evolved from a niche subculture into a pervasive social force, particularly in China, where fan communities constitute a significant part of internet users and have generated intense public debate (Zhang, 2024). Researchers have increasingly sought to understand why individuals form intense, one-sided attachments to media figures, and how these attachments affect their lives. This review synthesizes existing scholarship across three interconnected themes—the motivational foundations of celebrity worship, its perceived impact on personal well-being, and the sociocultural dynamics of Chinese fan culture—each informing the question of how psychological motivations shape university students' experiences of idol worship.

docs.title: Motivational Foundations of Celebrity Worship
docs.text:
The theoretical foundation for understanding idol worship lies in parasocial interaction, first articulated by Horton and Wohl (1956), who described the "intimacy at a distance" that audiences develop with media personalities. Building on this, McCutcheon, Lange, and Houran (2002) proposed the absorption-addiction model and developed the Celebrity Worship Scale (CWS), identifying three progressive stages: entertainment-social, intense-personal, and borderline-pathological worship. This framework has become the dominant paradigm in celebrity worship research.

Recent scholarship has explored specific psychological drivers of worship. Attachment theory has emerged as a particularly productive lens: Gong et al. (2025) demonstrated through a four-wave longitudinal study of 662 Chinese university freshmen that insecure attachment predicted higher initial celebrity worship and slowed its natural decline during the university transition, mediated by reduced self-concept clarity. MacNeill and DiTommaso (2022) similarly reported that anxiously attached individuals tend to form stronger parasocial relationships.

Complementing this, Jia et al. (2023) found that social anxiety positively predicted celebrity worship among 1,147 Chinese university students, with mobile phone dependence as a mediator—an effect amplified among students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

docs.title: Perceived Impact on Personal Well-Being
docs.text:
The relationship between celebrity worship and mental health has received considerable empirical attention. Maltby et al. (2004) established that intense-personal worship was associated with poorer mental health through neuroticism and disengagement coping. Brooks (2021) systematically reviewed 62 studies, confirming associations between celebrity worship and personality, self-esteem, and well-being, while noting that different motivational profiles remain underexplored.

More recent work has complicated this predominantly pathological picture. Wei and Huang (2024) employed latent profile analysis with 806 Chinese emerging adults and identified six distinct fan types, challenging the assumption of a simple healthy-to-pathological continuum. Zhang et al. (2026) used network analysis on 1,103 Chinese undergraduates and found that borderline-pathological worship cognition was the key bridge node connecting celebrity worship to reduced life satisfaction. However, Qu and Zhong (2025) revealed a parallel positive pathway: among 547 Chinese fans, anxious attachment indirectly predicted prosocial behavior through a chain mediation of parasocial intimacy and perceived social support. Morgan et al. (2024) further documented dual effects in qualitative research, showing that celebrity worship could both motivate personal growth and exacerbate anxiety.

docs.title: Chinese Fan Culture and Sociocultural Dynamics
docs.text: Understanding Chinese university students' idol worship requires situating individual psychology within broader sociocultural dynamics. Mao, Wang, and Chen (2023) characterized Chinese fan circles as quasi-organizational communities governed by emotional discipline and commercial logic, simultaneously celebrated for philanthropic activities and condemned for disruptive behavior. Zhang (2024) traced the 30-year evolution of China's online fandom, illustrating how platform architectures have shaped fan mobilization. Wang and Ge (2023) examined how fan communities interact with state power, revealing entanglements of fan identity, censorship sensibilities, and collective mobilization. Addressing measurement needs in the Chinese context, He and Liu (2024) developed a 36-item, nine-factor scale capturing excessive worship behaviors including social dysfunction, withdrawal, and escapism.

docs.title: Conclusion and Research Gaps
docs.text: Taken together, the literature reveals a rich but uneven landscape. While celebrity worship is well measured and its mental health correlates extensively documented, motivation has largely been treated as an implicit variable rather than a central object of inquiry. There is a scarcity of research on how distinct psychological motivations—such as attachment needs, social compensation, or aspirational identification—differentially relate to students' own perceptions of idol worship's impact on their personal lives. Furthermore, little has been done to examine how these motivational profiles shape students' perceptions of fan culture conflicts and their effect on the online environment. To date, few studies have connected individual psychological drivers to the collective dynamics of Chinese fan culture, leaving a critical gap in understanding how and why idol worship matters to those who practice it.

docs.title: 3.1 Research Design
docs.text: This study employed a cross-sectional online survey design to investigate the psychological motivations for idol worship among Chinese university students and their perceived impact at both personal and social levels. The survey instrument was designed to capture demographic information, idol worship status, motivational profiles, fan circle participation behaviors, and perceived impacts through a combination of multiple-choice, Likert-scale, and categorical items.

docs.title: 3.2 Participants
docs.text:
A total of 193 valid responses were collected from Chinese university students. The sample comprised 88 males (45.6%) and 105 females (54.4%). To ensure data quality, attention-check items were embedded in the questionnaire; responses failing these checks were excluded prior to analysis, which accounts for instances where the sum of response percentages in certain items falls below 100%.

Among all respondents, 56 (29.02%) reported actively worshipping and participating in fan activities, 73 (37.82%) reported having an idol but would not characterize their engagement as worship, and 64 (33.16%) reported no idol worship or fan circle involvement. Respondents who indicated having an idol (n = 129) proceeded to answer idol-specific questions regarding motivations, behaviors, and perceived impacts. The full sample (N = 193) was used for general perception questions answered by all respondents regardless of idol worship status.

docs.title: 3.3 Instrument
docs.text:
The survey instrument consisted of several sections designed to address the three research questions.

The first section collected demographic information including gender and idol worship status. The second section, administered to respondents with idols (n = 129), assessed idol type and information channels through multiple-choice items. Respondents could select from categories including entertainment celebrities, virtual idols, scholars and experts, sports stars, and internet celebrities, as well as information channels such as video platforms, social media, fan communities, and news websites.

The third section, core to RQ1, measured worship intensity using a self-rated 0–10 scale and assessed primary psychological motivations through a multiple-choice item with nine options. These options were inductively categorized into four motivational profiles: cognitive-aspirational (talent/ability recognition, personality/values identification, growth story/perseverance narrative, positive social influence), aesthetic attraction (appearance/image), emotional-attachment (emotional attachment/comfort, escapism), and social-belonging (social needs/connecting with others). An additional category, external influence (media or internet influence), was also included.

The fourth section assessed fan circle participation behaviors, including participation in organized activities (e.g., voting, purchasing merchandise) and involvement in idol/fan-circle conflict-related behavior, using categorical and multiple-choice items.

The fifth section, core to RQ2, measured perceived personal impact through a categorical item assessing overall perceived impact (positive, no significant impact, negative, or other) and four Likert-type items (1–5 scale) assessing specific impact dimensions including stress relief, values distortion, skill acquisition, and time displacement.

The sixth section, core to RQ3, measured perceived impact of idol/fan-circle conflicts on the online environment through separate items for idol-having respondents (n = 129) and non-idol respondents (n = 64), using a five-point categorical scale ranging from "seriously damaging" to "positive."

docs.title: 3.4 Data Analysis
docs.text: Given the descriptive and exploratory nature of this study, data analysis relied primarily on descriptive statistics, including frequency distributions, percentages, and mean scores. Motivational categories were derived through inductive grouping of survey response options based on their theoretical alignment with established psychological constructs. Cross-referencing between motivational data (Q6) and perceived impact data (Q11, Q12, Q13, Q14) was conducted to examine patterns of association between motivational profiles and perceived outcomes. No inferential statistical tests were performed, as the study aimed to identify and describe motivational patterns and their perceived correlates rather than to establish causal relationships.

docs.title: 4.1 RQ1: Dominant Psychological Motivations
docs.text:
The self-rated worship intensity among respondents with idols (n = 129) yielded a mean score of 6.19 on a 0–10 scale, indicating a moderate level of engagement. The distribution of primary motivations for idol worship revealed a clear hierarchy of psychological drivers, which were inductively categorized into five motivational profiles.

Cognitive-aspirational motivations emerged as the dominant cluster. Talent and ability recognition was the most frequently cited motivation, selected by 116 respondents (89.92%), followed closely by personality and values identification at 107 respondents (82.95%). The idol's growth story and perseverance narrative was selected by 61 respondents (47.29%), and the idol's positive social influence by 42 respondents (32.56%). Taken together, these cognitive-aspirational items consistently ranked among the highest-endorsed motivations, indicating that admiration for an idol's competence, character, and inspiring life trajectory constitutes the primary psychological driver of worship among Chinese university students.

Aesthetic attraction, measured by the appearance/image item, was a strong secondary factor, selected by 90 respondents (69.77%). This finding indicates that while visual appeal plays a substantial role, it is secondary to cognitive and aspirational considerations.

Emotional-attachment motivations were moderately prevalent. Emotional attachment and comfort were selected by 52 respondents (40.31%), while escapism was endorsed by only 12 respondents (9.30%). This pattern suggests that while a meaningful minority of students derive emotional sustenance from idol worship, the majority do not characterize their engagement in terms of emotional dependence or reality avoidance.

Social-belonging motivations, represented by social needs and connecting with others, were selected by only 15 respondents (11.63%), indicating that interpersonal and communal motivations are relatively minor drivers of idol worship in this sample.

External influence, represented by media or internet influence, was cited by 17 respondents (13.18%), suggesting that most students perceive their worship as internally motivated rather than externally imposed.

Regarding idol types, entertainment celebrities (singers, actors) were the most common, selected by 81 respondents (62.79%), followed by virtual idols (27.13%), scholars and experts (24.03%), sports stars (22.48%), and internet celebrities (13.18%). Video platforms (79.07%) and social media (76.74%) were the dominant information channels, with fan communities (30.23%) and news websites (18.60%) playing secondary roles.

docs.title: 4.2 RQ2: Perceived Personal Impact
docs.text:
The overall perceived impact of idol worship on university life among respondents with idols (n = 129) was predominantly positive or neutral. A total of 55 respondents (42.64%) reported a positive impact, 62 (48.06%) reported no significant impact, and only 7 (5.43%) perceived a negative impact. An additional 5 respondents (3.88%) described unique personal experiences that did not fit neatly into these categories.

The Likert-type items (1–5 scale) measuring specific impact dimensions revealed a nuanced picture. The item "excessive idol worship leads to distortion of personal values" received the highest mean score of 3.99, indicating relatively strong agreement and suggesting that even students who personally experience worship positively remain aware of its potential risks when taken to excess. By contrast, the items "idol worship helps students relieve stress and provides emotional comfort" (mean = 2.83) and "idol worship helps students learn new skills such as video editing and drawing" (mean = 2.78) received moderate scores, reflecting a cautious acknowledgment of potential benefits. The item "idol worship takes up a large amount of students' study and rest time" received the lowest mean score of 2.46, indicating that most students do not perceive worship as a significant time burden.

It is notable that these Likert items were framed from a bystander perspective—asking respondents about idol worship's impact on students in general rather than on themselves personally. The discrepancy between the positive self-reported overall impact (42.64% positive) and the relatively high concern about values distortion (mean = 3.99) suggests that students tend to evaluate their own worship experience more favorably than they evaluate the phenomenon in the abstract, a pattern consistent with optimistic self-assessment biases documented in social psychology.

docs.title: 4.3 RQ3: Perceived Impact on the Online Environment
docs.text:
A pronounced private–public paradox emerged when comparing students' personal worship experiences with their evaluations of fan culture's impact on the online environment.

Among respondents with idols (n = 129), 60 (46.51%) described fan-circle conflicts as "seriously damaging—full of hostility and malice," and an additional 31 (24.03%) rated the impact as "somewhat negative—but moderate in degree." Thus, over 70% of idol-worshipping respondents themselves evaluated fan culture conflicts as harmful to the online environment. Only 21 (16.28%) expressed indifference, 12 (9.30%) considered the conflicts neutral, and 5 (3.88%) perceived them as positive.

Among respondents without idols (n = 64), a similarly negative pattern was observed, though slightly less intense: 24 (37.50%) selected "seriously damaging," 22 (34.38%) selected "somewhat negative," 11 (17.19%) expressed indifference, 5 (7.81%) considered the conflicts neutral, and 2 (3.13%) perceived them as positive. In total, 71.88% of non-idol respondents evaluated the impact as negative.

The convergence between the two groups is striking: both idol-worshipping and non-idol respondents overwhelmingly perceived fan culture conflicts as damaging to the online environment. However, the idol-worshipping group showed an even higher proportion selecting the most extreme negative category (46.51% vs. 37.50%), suggesting that direct familiarity with fan culture may heighten rather than diminish awareness of its destructive collective dynamics.

This finding stands in sharp contrast to the personal impact data reported in Section 4.2, where 90.70% of idol-worshipping respondents perceived their personal worship experience as positive or neutral. The tension between positive personal experience and negative public evaluation constitutes the central empirical paradox of this study.

docs.title: 4.4 Supplementary Findings: Fan Circle Participation and Conflict Behavior
docs.text:
To provide additional context for interpreting the above findings, the survey also assessed fan circle participation behaviors and conflict involvement.

Among respondents with idols, only 34 (26.36%) confirmed participation in organized fan-circle activities, with an additional 14 (10.85%) indicating probable participation. Among those who had participated (n = 59), the most common activities were purchasing idol merchandise (77.97%), participating in online discussions (61.02%), attending offline events (37.29%), voting and chart ranking (32.20%), and creating promotional content (30.51%).

Regarding conflict behavior, 19 respondents (14.73%) acknowledged participation in idol/fan-circle conflict-related behavior, while 99 (76.74%) denied such participation and 11 (8.53%) preferred not to say. Among those who acknowledged conflict participation (n = 19), the most common forms were arguing in comment sections or private messages (63.16%), reposting or spreading content to support one's side (31.58%), posting complaints about opposing idols or fans (26.32%), and participating in organized counter-attacks or comment flooding (21.05%).

These supplementary findings suggest that while the majority of idol-worshipping students engage with their idols through personal media consumption rather than organized collective action, a significant minority does participate in the organized dynamics that both groups perceive as harmful to the online environment.

docs.title: 5.1 Cognitive-Aspirational Dominance and Higher-Order Needs
docs.text:
The most striking finding of this study is the overwhelming dominance of cognitive-aspirational motivations. With talent recognition endorsed by 89.92% and values identification by 82.95% of respondents with idols, the motivational landscape is clearly anchored in admiration for competence and character rather than in emotional dependence, social pressure, or escapism. This pattern aligns with Maslow's hierarchy of needs at the levels of esteem and self-actualization: students appear to engage with idols as aspirational models who embody qualities they themselves value and wish to cultivate. The relatively low endorsement of escapism (9.30%) and social-belonging needs (11.63%) challenges the assumption, prevalent in both popular discourse and some academic literature, that idol worship is primarily a compensatory behavior driven by psychological deficiency.

This finding complements Wei and Huang's (2024) identification of six distinct fan profiles among Chinese emerging adults. While their latent profile analysis revealed heterogeneity in fan engagement, the present study's motivation-centered approach demonstrates that across this heterogeneity, cognitive-aspirational drivers remain the common foundation. The result is also consistent with Morgan et al.'s (2024) qualitative finding that celebrity worship can inspire personal growth alongside its potential to exacerbate anxiety—our data suggest that the growth-inspiring dimension is far more prevalent in this sample than the anxiety-exacerbating one.

docs.title: 5.2 The Private–Public Paradox
docs.text:
The second major finding is the sharp disconnect between students' evaluations of their personal worship experience and their evaluations of fan culture's collective impact on the online environment. While 90.70% of idol-worshipping respondents described their personal experience as positive or neutral, over 70% of the same group characterized fan-circle conflicts as damaging to the online environment, with 46.51% selecting the most extreme negative category. This private–public paradox is the central empirical contribution of this study.

The paradox indicates that the transition from individual admiration to organized fan participation introduces qualitatively different risks. Mao et al. (2023) offer a useful framework for understanding this transition: they characterized Chinese fan circles as "quasi-organizational communities" governed by emotional discipline and commercial logic. Within these structures, individual attachment is channeled into collective actions such as chart-ranking campaigns, coordinated comment flooding, and consumption-based loyalty tests. Zhang (2024) traced how 30 years of online fandom evolution in China have progressively strengthened these organizational capacities, turning casual fan groups into highly mobilized communities capable of large-scale coordinated action. The present study's finding that even the students who personally benefit from idol worship recognize its collective destructiveness supports this structural interpretation: the problem is not individual admiration but the organizational systems that transform it into collective conflict.

The Likert-scale results further illuminate this tension. Students' relatively strong agreement with the statement that excessive idol worship leads to distortion of personal values (mean = 3.99) coexists with their predominantly positive self-assessment of personal impact. This discrepancy may reflect an optimistic self-assessment bias—the tendency to evaluate one's own situation more favorably than the general case—but it may also indicate genuine differentiation: students may accurately perceive that their own moderate engagement is beneficial while recognizing that the organized dynamics of fan culture produce harmful outcomes at the collective level.

docs.title: 5.3 Individual Worship Versus Organized Fan Culture
docs.text:
Idol worship among university students is frequently discussed as though it were a single, inherently harmful behavior. Critics point to excessive consumption, wasted time, and emotional dependence, treating anyone who admires a celebrity as a potential victim of psychological damage. However, this view oversimplifies a complex phenomenon. Drawing on both existing scholarship and original survey data collected from 193 Chinese university students, the evidence from this study argues that idol worship itself is not the primary threat to students' well-being. Rather, the real danger lies in the organized, collective dynamics of fan culture—the systems of group pressure, commercial manipulation, and online conflict that transform a largely positive personal experience into a socially destructive force.

First, the evidence suggests that individual idol worship is, for most students, a neutral or even beneficial activity. Our survey data show that 42.6% of respondents reported a positive effect of idol worship on university life, 48.1% reported no significant effect, and only 5.4% perceived a negative impact. The dominant motivations are cognitive and aspirational: 89.9% cited talent recognition as a primary reason, and 83.0% identified with the idol's personality or values. By contrast, escapism was selected by only 9.3%, and social needs by just 11.6%. These figures challenge the assumption that idol worship is driven by emotional dependence. Instead, most students engage in worship as aspirational identification, aligning with Maslow's hierarchy at the levels of esteem and self-actualization. This interpretation is supported by Morgan et al. (2024), whose qualitative study documented dual effects of celebrity worship, finding it could both inspire personal growth and exacerbate anxiety. Qu and Zhong (2025) also demonstrated a positive pathway, showing that idol worship among Chinese fans could promote prosocial behavior through parasocial intimacy and perceived social support. The individual experience of admiring an idol is not inherently harmful—it can function as an emotional resource.

However, the picture changes dramatically when we shift from the individual to the collective level. Our data uncover a striking paradox: while most students view their personal worship positively, they hold deeply negative views about fan culture's impact on the online environment. Specifically, 46.5% of respondents with idols described fan-circle conflicts as "seriously damaging, full of hostility and malice," and an additional 24.0% rated the impact as "somewhat negative." Over 70% of idol-worshipping students themselves recognize that collective fan behavior harms public online spaces. This private–public paradox is crucial. It indicates that the transition from individual admiration to organized fan participation introduces qualitatively different risks. Mao et al. (2023) offer a useful framework: they characterize Chinese fan circles as "quasi-organizational communities" relying on emotional discipline and commercial logic. Within these structures, individual attachment is channeled into collective actions such as chart-ranking campaigns, coordinated comment flooding, and consumption-based loyalty tests. Zhang (2024) traced how 30 years of online fandom evolution in China have progressively strengthened these organizational capacities, turning casual fan groups into highly mobilized communities capable of large-scale coordinated action.

Some might argue that individual and collective worship cannot be meaningfully separated—that once a person admires an idol, they inevitably become drawn into fan culture's organized dynamics. While there is some truth to this concern, our data suggest otherwise. Among respondents who reported having an idol, only 26.4% had participated in organized fan-circle activities such as voting or purchasing merchandise. The remaining majority engaged with their idols through personal media consumption without active collective participation. This finding is consistent with Wei and Huang (2024), who identified six distinct fan profiles among Chinese emerging adults, demonstrating that fan engagement exists along a spectrum rather than as a binary. Moreover, Zhang et al. (2026) used network analysis to show that it is specifically borderline-pathological worship cognition—not moderate admiration—that serves as the key bridge connecting celebrity worship to reduced life satisfaction. In other words, there is a clear threshold between healthy individual engagement and the kind of excessive, group-driven involvement that causes harm.

docs.title: 5.4 Connections to Prior Research
docs.text:
The present findings resonate with and extend several key contributions in the literature. McCutcheon et al.'s (2002) three-stage model (entertainment-social, intense-personal, borderline-pathological) implies that most worship occurs at the milder end of the continuum. Our motivational data support this implication: the dominance of cognitive-aspirational motivations and the low endorsement of escapism and emotional dependence suggest that the majority of worship in our sample corresponds to the entertainment-social stage, characterized by curiosity, admiration, and voluntary engagement rather than compulsion or distress.

The finding that personal impact perceptions are predominantly positive is consistent with Qu and Zhong's (2025) demonstration of a positive pathway from idol worship to prosocial behavior, mediated by parasocial intimacy and perceived social support. It also aligns with Gong et al.'s (2025) observation that celebrity worship naturally declines during the university transition for securely attached students, suggesting that for most students, idol worship is a developmental phase rather than a persistent pathology.

At the same time, our finding that students themselves recognize fan culture's destructive collective impact is consistent with Zhang et al.'s (2026) network analysis showing that borderline-pathological worship cognition serves as the bridge to reduced life satisfaction. The implication is that the boundary between beneficial and harmful worship is real and perceptible to students themselves—even if they do not always recognize when they are crossing it. Wang and Ge's (2023) analysis of fan conflicts and state power in China further contextualizes our finding: the organized dynamics that students perceive as harmful do not arise spontaneously from individual admiration but are shaped by platform architectures, commercial incentives, and the political environment.

docs.title: 5.5 Limitations and Future Research
docs.text:
Several limitations should be acknowledged. First, the sample size (N = 193) limits generalizability. Second, all measures were self-reported and therefore susceptible to social desirability bias, particularly regarding conflict participation and negative impact acknowledgment. Third, the cross-sectional design precludes causal inference; we can identify associations between motivational profiles and perceived impacts but cannot determine directionality. Fourth, the use of descriptive statistics without inferential testing limits the strength of conclusions that can be drawn about group differences. Fifth, the motivational categories were derived inductively from survey items rather than from a validated scale, which may affect construct validity.

Future research should address these limitations through larger and more representative samples, longitudinal designs that can track how motivations and perceptions change over time, validated motivational scales adapted to the Chinese context, and mixed-methods approaches that combine survey data with qualitative interviews to capture the complexity of students' lived experiences. Additionally, experimental or quasi-experimental designs could help isolate the specific mechanisms through which organized fan culture dynamics transform individual admiration into collective harm.

docs.title: 6. Conclusion
docs.text:
This study set out to investigate the psychological motivations driving idol worship among Chinese university students and how these motivations relate to perceived impacts at both personal and social levels. Three key findings emerged.

First, cognitive-aspirational motivations—particularly talent recognition and personality/values identification—dominate the motivational landscape of idol worship among Chinese university students, with talent and character recognition far exceeding emotional dependence or social needs as primary drivers. This finding challenges the prevailing narrative that idol worship is primarily driven by psychological deficiency and supports an interpretation grounded in Maslow's higher-order needs for esteem and self-actualization.

Second, the perceived personal impact of idol worship is largely positive or neutral. The overwhelming majority of students who have idols view their engagement as personally beneficial or inconsequential, suggesting that moderate idol worship can serve as an emotional and aspirational resource rather than a threat to well-being.

Third, a significant private–public paradox exists: while students view their personal worship experience positively, they simultaneously acknowledge that fan culture conflicts are damaging to the online environment. This paradox points to a critical distinction between individual idol worship and organized fan culture. The evidence from this study, supported by the broader literature, consistently indicates that the genuine risks emerge not from individual admiration but from the collective, organized dynamics of fan culture—the systems of emotional discipline, commercial exploitation, and coordinated conflict that transform a largely positive personal experience into a socially destructive force.

In conclusion, treating idol worship as a monolithic threat to university students is both inaccurate and counterproductive. Educational and policy responses should focus not on discouraging idol worship itself, but on equipping students with the media literacy and critical thinking skills needed to recognize when individual admiration is being co-opted by collective dynamics that no longer serve their interests. The data from this study suggest that students themselves already perceive this distinction—the task for educators is to help them act on it.
